name: Carpenter
summary: A woodworker who cuts, joins, and erects timber structures—from medieval cathedral scaffolding to modern house framing and finish carpentry.
duties: Measure and cut lumber, assemble frames, install doors and trim, read plans, and repair wooden structures in buildings and ships.
skills: Joinery, geometry, material selection, tool sharpening, blueprint literacy in modern work.
tools: Hand saws, chisels, planes, mallets; modern circular saws, nail guns, laser levels.
regions: Worldwide; essential in agrarian, maritime, and urban construction economies.
originLabel: Early timber construction in settled societies
educationPath: Apprenticeship, trade school, or union carpenter training programs.
modernSectors: Residential construction, commercial build-outs, set construction, heritage restoration.

events.label: Industrial milling
events.description: Machine-cut lumber standardizes dimensions and scales suburban housing.

sources.title: Building construction histories
sources.note: Timber framing attested across ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia.

name: Mason
summary: A builder who lays stone, brick, or concrete to form walls, vaults, roads, and monuments—one of the oldest skilled construction trades.
duties: Prepare mortar, set courses of brick or stone, build arches and foundations, interpret structural plans, and restore historic masonry.
skills: Bond patterns, load awareness, mortar mixing, precision layout, safety on scaffolding.
tools: Trowels, levels, plumb bobs, hammers; modern concrete mixers and laser alignment.
regions: Global; dominant in Mediterranean, European, Islamic, and colonial architecture.
educationPath: Masonry apprenticeship and trade certification.
modernSectors: Construction, infrastructure, monument conservation.

events.label: Gothic building boom
events.description: Cathedral programs employ large mason lodges across Europe.

sources.title: Architectural history surveys
sources.note: Stone dressing predates written craft manuals.

name: Wheelwright
summary: A craft worker who built and repaired wooden wheels for carts, carriages, and early vehicles, combining carpentry with iron tire fitting.
duties: Shape fellies and spokes, fit hubs, shrink iron tires onto rims, and maintain wagon fleets for farms and transport companies.
skills: Wood bending, hub boring, ironwork coordination, vehicle dynamics.
tools: Wheel lathe, travis, hoop driver, spoke shave.
regions: Europe and North America through the early 20th century.

events.label: Automobile dominance
events.description: Rubber-tired motor vehicles replace horse-drawn wheel demand in cities.

name: Tailor
summary: A maker and fitter of custom clothing who cuts fabric to measure—distinct from industrial garment workers in the bespoke tradition.
duties: Take measurements, draft patterns, sew garments, alter finished clothing, and advise clients on textiles.
skills: Pattern drafting, hand and machine sewing, fitting, textile knowledge.
tools: Shears, needles, sewing machine, pressing iron, mannequin.
regions: Urban centers worldwide; strong traditions in London, Naples, Hong Kong, and Delhi.
originLabel: Urban guild tailoring in medieval Europe
educationPath: Apprenticeship or fashion school programs.
modernSectors: Bespoke menswear, bridal, costume, uniform tailoring.

events.label: Ready-made expansion
events.description: Sized ready-to-wear competes with custom tailoring for middle-class buyers.

name: Weaver
summary: A textile worker who interlaces warp and weft threads on a loom to produce cloth—foundational to pre-industrial economies and still present in artisan and industrial mills.
duties: Set up looms, monitor tension, repair broken threads, and meet cloth quality standards for household or commercial buyers.
skills: Pattern reading, loom maintenance, fiber handling, color matching.
tools: Hand loom, shuttle, heddles; modern power looms and Jacquard heads.
regions: South Asia, Middle East, Europe, and industrial mill towns globally.

events.label: Mechanized spinning and weaving
events.description: Factory systems transform output in Britain and export globally.

name: Miller
summary: An operator of mills who grinds grain into flour using water, wind, or mechanical power—central to food supply chains before industrial food processing.
duties: Receive grain, adjust millstones, bag flour, maintain machinery, and manage dust safety.
skills: Grain grading, machinery tuning, bookkeeping, hydraulics or wind sail adjustment.
tools: Millstones, bolters, waterwheels or turbines, modern roller mills.
regions: Rural communities worldwide; windmill landscapes in Netherlands and grain belts in North America.

events.label: Roller milling
events.description: Steel rollers produce consistent white flour at industrial scale.

name: Tanner
summary: A worker who converts animal hides into leather through curing, soaking, and tanning agents—supplying material for shoes, saddles, and harnesses.
duties: Flesh hides, treat with tannins or chemicals, stretch and finish leather, manage effluent and odor controls.
skills: Chemistry awareness, hide grading, tool maintenance, regulatory compliance.
tools: Tanning vats, fleshing knives, stretching frames; modern drum tanners.
regions: Historic tannery districts in Morocco, Italy, and New England; global export today.
originLabel: Leather goods in Bronze Age trade

events.label: Chrome tanning
events.description: Faster chemical processes displace many traditional vegetable tanneries.

name: Butcher
summary: A meat trade worker who slaughters, dresses, and cuts animals into retail portions—present from ancient market streets to modern cold-chain plants.
duties: Break down carcasses, trim cuts, maintain hygiene standards, serve customers, and manage cold storage.
skills: Knife work, food safety, customer service, inventory rotation.
tools: Cleaver, boning knife, band saw, refrigeration, cutting blocks.
regions: Worldwide wherever livestock and meat consumption exist.
originLabel: Urban meat markets in early cities
educationPath: Butchery apprenticeship or culinary meat fabrication training.
modernSectors: Retail counters, wholesale packing, restaurant butchery programs.

events.label: Industrial meat inspection
events.description: Urban packing plants spur standardized slaughter and regulation in the U.S.

name: Baker
summary: A food worker who mixes, proofs, and bakes bread and pastries for households, institutions, and commercial supply.
duties: Prepare dough, operate ovens, decorate goods, manage fermentation schedules, and maintain food safety.
skills: Recipe scaling, fermentation control, time management, retail service.
tools: Ovens, proofing cabinets, mixers, peels, rolling pins.
regions: Global; every settled agricultural society.
originLabel: Early grain baking in Mesopotamia and Egypt
educationPath: Culinary school, apprenticeship, or in-house training.
modernSectors: Retail bakeries, supermarket production, hotel pastry, artisan sourdough.

events.label: Commercial bakeries
events.description: Urbanization supports dedicated bakery chains and mechanized mixing.

name: Printer
summary: A worker who reproduces text and images—first as a craft of movable type and later as an industrial and digital reproduction trade.
duties: Set type or prepare plates, operate presses, check proofs, maintain ink systems, and manage print runs.
skills: Typography, color matching, press mechanics, file prepress in modern work.
tools: Composing stick, press, lithographic plates; digital RIP workflows today.
regions: European print centers (Mainz, Venice), colonial presses, global publishing hubs.

events.label: Gutenberg Bible
events.description: Demonstrates durable movable-type book production in Europe.

events.label: Offset lithography
events.description: High-volume illustrated printing transforms newspapers and advertising.

name: Bookkeeper
summary: A clerk who maintains financial records—ledgers, invoices, and reconciliations—for households, firms, and institutions.
duties: Record transactions, reconcile accounts, prepare reports for accountants, and manage payroll data entry.
skills: Numeracy, double-entry literacy, spreadsheet fluency, attention to detail.
tools: Ledgers historically; accounting software and ERP modules today.
regions: Commercial economies worldwide since medieval merchant houses.
educationPath: Associate degrees, bookkeeping certificates, on-the-job training.
modernSectors: SMB finance, nonprofit admin, franchise back-office.

events.label: Spreadsheet software
events.description: VisiCalc and successors shift bookkeeping from paper ledgers to digital models.

name: Secretary
summary: An administrative professional who manages correspondence, schedules, and office workflow—evolving from confidential clerks to executive support roles.
duties: Draft letters, arrange meetings, maintain files, gatekeep communications, and coordinate travel.
skills: Writing, discretion, software literacy, calendar management, stakeholder tact.
tools: Typewriters historically; email, calendars, and collaboration suites today.
regions: Government ministries, corporate offices, legal and medical practices globally.
originLabel: Confidential clerks to nobility and merchants
educationPath: Business administration courses and vocational office training.
modernSectors: Corporate HQs, law firms, medical practices, nonprofits.

events.label: Office mechanization
events.description: Photocopiers and dictation machines reshape secretarial workflows.

name: Accountant
summary: A finance professional who prepares, audits, and interprets accounts—evolving from merchant reckoning to certified public practice and corporate finance.
duties: Prepare financial statements, ensure tax compliance, advise on controls, and audit books.
skills: Accounting standards, analytics, regulatory knowledge, client communication.
tools: Ledgers, calculators, audit software, tax engines.
regions: Global; professional bodies in UK, US, India, and beyond.
educationPath: Accounting degrees, CPA/chartered exams, continuing education.
modernSectors: Public practice, corporate FP&A, government, forensic audit.

events.label: Professional institutes
events.description: Formal accounting bodies emerge in Scotland and spread globally.

name: Physician
summary: A trained healer who diagnoses illness and prescribes treatment—evolving from humoral theory and apprenticeship to evidence-based medical licensure.
duties: Examine patients, order tests, prescribe therapies, coordinate care teams, and maintain clinical records.
skills: Diagnosis, pharmacology, communication, ethics, continuous medical education.
tools: Stethoscope, imaging systems, EHR software; historically lancets and herbals.
regions: Global; institutional medicine rooted in Greek, Islamic, and European schools.
educationPath: Medical school, residency, board certification.
modernSectors: Hospitals, primary care, specialties, public health agencies.

events.label: Scientific medicine
events.description: Germ theory and laboratory science reshape training and hospitals.

name: Midwife
summary: A birth attendant who supports labor, delivery, and postpartum care—one of humanity's oldest specialized roles, now split between traditional and certified practice.
duties: Monitor pregnancies, assist deliveries, provide neonatal care, educate families, and refer complications to physicians.
skills: Clinical assessment, calm under pressure, cultural sensitivity, emergency protocols.
tools: Sterile kits, fetal dopplers, suturing supplies; historically hands and herbal aids.
regions: Worldwide; strong community traditions in Africa, Latin America, and Europe.
originLabel: Attested birth attendants in early societies
educationPath: Certified nurse-midwife programs or traditional apprenticeship pathways.
modernSectors: Hospitals, birth centers, home birth practices, public health.

events.label: Professional midwifery schools
events.description: Formal training expands in Europe and North America amid medicalization debates.

name: Apothecary
summary: A preparer and seller of medicines and compounds before the modern split between pharmacists and physicians—central to early modern town commerce.
duties: Compound tinctures and pills, advise on dosages, stock herbs and chemicals, and keep shop accounts.
skills: Botanical knowledge, measurement, retail trade, basic diagnosis in some traditions.
tools: Mortar and pestle, scales, jars, distillation apparatus.
regions: European high streets and colonial ports through the 19th century.
originLabel: Urban pharmacy shops in medieval Europe

events.label: Regulated pharmacy
events.description: Many jurisdictions require formal qualifications, separating apothecary folklore from licensed pharmacy.

name: Nurse
summary: A caregiver who tends the sick and injured in hospitals, homes, and battlefields—professionalized from religious and domestic service into licensed clinical practice.
duties: Administer treatments, monitor vitals, coordinate care plans, educate patients, and document outcomes.
skills: Clinical procedures, empathy, teamwork, infection control, advocacy.
tools: IV lines, monitors, EHRs; historically basins, bandages, lanterns.
regions: Global; Nightingale-era reforms spread standardized training.
originLabel: Modern nursing schools after Crimean War reforms
educationPath: Nursing degrees, licensure exams, specialty certifications.
modernSectors: Hospitals, home health, schools, military, public health.

events.label: Nightingale training school
events.description: Formal nursing education begins at St Thomas' Hospital, London.

name: Knight
summary: A mounted warrior granted land or pay in exchange for military service and feudal obligations—a defining medieval European institution, not a modern job title.
duties: Train for combat, serve liege lords in war, administer estates, and uphold chivalric codes in court life.
skills: Horsemanship, arms drill, tactics, heraldry, estate management.
tools: Sword, lance, armor, warhorse, castle garrison equipment.
regions: Medieval and early modern Europe; parallels in other warrior-elite systems.

events.label: Gunpowder warfare
events.description: Massed infantry and cannon reduce the strategic dominance of heavy cavalry.

name: Archer
summary: A soldier or hunter skilled with bow and arrow—decisive on medieval battlefields and in siege warfare before firearms dominance.
duties: Loose volleys in formation, snipe in skirmishes, maintain bows and fletching, and train continuously for draw strength.
skills: Aim, unit discipline, equipment care, fieldcraft.
tools: Longbow, composite bow, crossbow, arrows, bracers.
regions: England, Wales, East Asia, Middle East, and indigenous Americas.
originLabel: Bow warfare in Bronze Age armies

events.label: Firearms adoption
events.description: Handgonnes and arquebuses displace bow volleys in many European armies.

name: Sailor
summary: A seafarer who crews ships for fishing, trade, exploration, and naval service—one of the world's oldest wage occupations.
duties: Handle rigging, navigate, maintain hull and engines, load cargo, and stand watches at sea.
skills: Knotwork, weather reading, teamwork, mechanical repair, safety drills.
tools: Sextant historically; modern radar, GPS, and diesel or sail systems.
regions: Coastal and river economies worldwide.
educationPath: Maritime academies, merchant marine licenses, naval training.
modernSectors: Container shipping, fishing fleets, cruise lines, offshore energy.

events.label: Atlantic age of sail
events.description: Transoceanic routes expand crew demand and navigation science.

name: Electrician
summary: A tradesperson who installs and maintains electrical power, lighting, and control systems—emerging with urban electrification in the late 19th century.
duties: Wire buildings, troubleshoot circuits, read blueprints, ensure code compliance, and upgrade panels.
skills: Circuit theory, safety codes, conduit bending, problem diagnosis.
tools: Multimeter, wire strippers, conduit benders, insulated hand tools.
regions: Industrialized and electrifying economies worldwide since ~1880.
educationPath: Electrical apprenticeship, journeyman licensing, continuing code education.
modernSectors: Construction, maintenance, renewables, data centers.

events.label: Grid expansion
events.description: City-wide power distribution creates sustained demand for wiring trades.

name: Mechanic
summary: A repair specialist for machines—originally mill and steam equipment, now strongly associated with automobiles and industrial plant maintenance.
duties: Diagnose faults, replace parts, tune engines, document repairs, and advise customers or operators.
skills: Mechanical reasoning, tool proficiency, diagnostics, customer communication.
tools: Wrenches, lifts, OBD scanners, torque wrenches.
regions: Global wherever mechanized transport and industry exist.
educationPath: Trade school, ASE certifications, manufacturer training.
modernSectors: Auto repair, fleet maintenance, aviation MRO, factory upkeep.

events.label: Mass automobility
events.description: Model T era expands neighborhood garages and mechanic employment.

name: Pilot
summary: An aviator who commands aircraft for passenger, cargo, military, or agricultural operations—born in the 20th-century aviation industry.
duties: Preflight checks, navigate airways, communicate with control, manage crew, and respond to emergencies.
skills: Aeronautics, instrument flying, crew resource management, regulation literacy.
tools: Flight deck instruments, autopilot, simulators, checklists.
regions: Global hubs and regional carriers since the 1910s.
originLabel: Early professional aviators after Wright flights
educationPath: Flight school, ATP licensure, type ratings, simulator recurrent training.
modernSectors: Airlines, cargo, charter, agriculture, military aviation.

events.label: Jet passenger era
events.description: Boeing 707 class jets mainstream intercontinental travel.

name: Journalist
summary: A gatherer and publisher of news and analysis for newspapers, broadcast, and digital platforms—professionalized alongside mass printing and telegraph wires.
duties: Report facts, interview sources, verify claims, write stories, and meet ethical and legal standards.
skills: Writing, research, media law awareness, multimedia production, speed under deadline.
tools: Notebook, camera, CMS, social platforms; historically telegraph and typewriter.
regions: Urban press centers worldwide; strong traditions in London, New York, and Delhi.
originLabel: Penny press and mass newspaper markets
educationPath: Journalism degrees, newsroom apprenticeships, portfolio building.
modernSectors: Digital news, broadcast, magazines, independent newsletters.

events.label: Telegraph news wires
events.description: Rapid long-distance reporting transforms national news desks.

name: Photographer
summary: A image-maker who captures photographs for art, journalism, science, and commerce—emerging with chemical photography in the 19th century.
duties: Compose shots, operate cameras, edit images, manage clients, and archive digital assets.
skills: Lighting, composition, color grading, client direction, equipment maintenance.
tools: Camera bodies, lenses, strobes, Lightroom; historically darkroom chemicals.
regions: Global studios and freelance markets since daguerreotype era.
educationPath: Art school, assistantships, portfolio and business skills.
modernSectors: Wedding, commercial, editorial, forensic, stock licensing.

events.label: Mass-market cameras
events.description: Kodak and competitors bring photography to amateur households.

name: Farmer
summary: A cultivator who grows crops and raises livestock for subsistence or market—among the oldest sustained occupations in human history.
duties: Prepare soil, plant and harvest, manage herds, maintain equipment, and market produce.
skills: Agronomy, animal husbandry, weather planning, machinery operation, business planning.
tools: Plow, sickle, tractor, irrigation; historically oxen and hand tools.
regions: Every agricultural region worldwide.
educationPath: Agricultural extension programs, family apprenticeship, agronomy degrees.
modernSectors: Row crops, livestock, organic markets, agtech-managed farms.

events.label: Mechanized farming
events.description: Tractors and combines reshape labor per acre in industrialized countries.

name: Shepherd
summary: A pastoral worker who tends sheep—guiding flocks to pasture, guarding from predators, and producing wool and milk in pre-industrial and modern highlands.
duties: Move flocks, assist lambing, shear wool, maintain enclosures, and sell animals at market.
skills: Animal reading, terrain knowledge, dog handling, weather sense.
tools: Crook, flute, sheepdogs, ATVs in modern hills.
regions: Mediterranean, British Isles, Middle East, Andes, and New Zealand.

events.label: Enclosure and industrial wool
events.description: Mechanized mills increase wool demand while reducing traditional open-field grazing.

name: Fisherman
summary: A harvester of fish and shellfish from rivers, lakes, and seas—critical to coastal diets and trade since prehistoric times.
duties: Deploy nets or lines, navigate small vessels, process catch, comply with quotas, and maintain gear.
skills: Navigation, knotwork, species identification, safety at sea, market timing.
tools: Nets, traps, rods, sonar; historically dugout canoes and weirs.
regions: Coastal villages worldwide; major fleets in East Asia and North Atlantic.
educationPath: Deckhand apprenticeship, maritime safety certifications.
modernSectors: Commercial trawling, aquaculture support, sport charter, subsistence fishing.

events.label: Industrial trawling
events.description: Factory ships and diesel engines intensify harvests and depletion debates.

name: Teacher
summary: An educator who instructs learners in schools, temples, and tutoring contexts—formalized from clerical tutors to licensed classroom professionals.
duties: Plan lessons, assess students, manage classrooms, communicate with families, and adapt materials.
skills: Pedagogy, subject mastery, classroom management, empathy, assessment design.
tools: Chalkboards historically; LMS, projectors, and tablets today.
regions: Every literate society; temple schools in antiquity to modern public systems.
originLabel: Temple and scribal schools in Mesopotamia
educationPath: Teacher certification, subject degrees, continuing professional development.
modernSectors: K-12 schools, universities, tutoring, corporate training.

events.label: Common school movements
events.description: Public education expansions professionalize teaching in Europe and North America.

name: Librarian
summary: A curator of collections who organizes knowledge resources and helps readers navigate catalogs—from monastic scriptoria to public and digital libraries.
duties: Catalog materials, assist research, manage lending, preserve archives, and teach information literacy.
skills: Classification systems, reference interviewing, preservation, digital search, programming events.
tools: Card catalogs historically; ILS software, scanners, databases today.
regions: Universities, municipalities, and special collections worldwide.
originLabel: Great Library of Alexandria era institutions
educationPath: MLIS degrees, archives certifications, subject specialization.
modernSectors: Public libraries, academic libraries, law libraries, digital repositories.

events.label: ALA founded
events.description: Professional library associations standardize training in the United States.

name: Gunner
summary: An artillery specialist who loaded and aimed cannon and early firearms in siege and field warfare—distinct from later technical ratings on naval vessels.
duties: Prepare powder charges, aim bombard pieces, calculate range, and maintain artillery trains.
skills: Ballistics intuition, teamwork under fire, equipment maintenance, drill discipline.
tools: Cannons, mortars, gun carriages, sponges and rammers.
regions: European and Ottoman siege warfare; shipboard gun crews globally.

events.label: Industrial artillery
events.description: Railway guns and massed shelling transform battlefield gunnery into specialized corps.

name: Candlemaker
summary: A artisan who produced candles from tallow or wax for household and ecclesiastical light before widespread kerosene and electric lighting.
duties: Render tallow, dip or mold wicks, scent and package candles, and supply chandlers' shops.
skills: Wick sizing, temperature control, retail trade, fragrance blending in modern craft.
tools: Dipping racks, molds, melting pots; boutique pour setups today.
regions: Europe and colonies through the 19th century; niche craft markets now.
originLabel: Tallow chandlery in Roman and medieval towns

events.label: Kerosene lamps
events.description: Mineral oil lamps reduce household candle demand in cities.

name: Glassblower
summary: A craft worker who shapes molten glass with a blowpipe into vessels, windows, and scientific apparatus.
duties: Gather molten glass, blow and shape forms, anneal pieces, and finish rims and stems.
skills: Heat timing, hand-eye coordination, design sense, teamwork in factory settings.
tools: Blowpipe, marver, punty, glory hole furnace.
regions: Venice, Bohemia, and industrial glass towns; studio art worldwide.

events.label: Machine glass
events.description: Automated bottle machines compete with hand blowing for utilitarian ware.

name: Miner
summary: A underground or open-pit worker who extracts ore, coal, and minerals—dangerous labor central to industrial revolutions and still vital for modern supply chains.
duties: Drill headings, shore tunnels, haul ore, operate ventilation systems, and follow safety protocols.
skills: Geology basics, machinery operation, teamwork, hazard awareness, endurance.
tools: Pick and shovel historically; continuous miners, drills, and haul trucks today.
regions: Coal districts, Andean silver mines, African copper belts, Appalachian coalfields.
educationPath: Mining engineering programs, trade certifications, safety training.
modernSectors: Coal, metals, rare earths, quarrying, potash.

events.label: Industrial coal demand
events.description: Steam engines and iron smelting intensify deep shaft mining in Britain.

name: Railroad Conductor
summary: A rail crew leader who manages train operations, tickets, and safety on passenger and freight runs—iconic to 19th-century mobility.
duties: Signal departures, verify consist paperwork, assist passengers, coordinate with engineers, and enforce rail rules.
skills: Procedure memorization, customer service, authority under schedule pressure, radio communication.
tools: Lanterns and flags historically; radios, tablets, and ticketing systems today.
regions: North America, Europe, India, and Japan rail networks.
educationPath: Railroad carrier training programs and federal certification (US).
modernSectors: Freight railroads, commuter rail, tourist heritage lines.

events.label: Transcontinental rail
events.description: US coast-to-coast line expands long-distance conductor employment.

name: Lighthouse Keeper
summary: A coastal warden who maintained lighthouses, lit beacons, and logged maritime weather—romanticized but often isolated government service.
duties: Trim wicks or maintain lamps, polish lenses, rescue castaways when possible, and keep station logs.
skills: Mechanical upkeep, watch discipline, Morse and later radio basics, endurance.
tools: Fresnel lenses, oil lamps, fog signals, logbooks.
regions: Atlantic and Pacific coastlines; British and US aid-to-navigation systems.

events.label: Automation
events.description: Solar-powered automated beacons replace most resident keepers.

name: Lamplighter
summary: A municipal worker who lit and extinguished street gas lamps each dusk and dawn—an everyday urban job erased by electric street lighting.
duties: Carry ladder and torch, open gas valves, clean mantles, report leaks, and maintain lamp posts on a route.
skills: Route memory, punctuality, basic gas safety, climbing.
tools: Ladder, lighting pole, gas key, mantles.
regions: Gas-lit cities in Europe and North America, 19th century peak.

events.label: Electric streetlights
events.description: Municipal electrification ends most gas lamp rounds.

name: Switchboard Operator
summary: A telephone exchange worker who manually connected calls by plugging cables into switchboards—central to early 20th-century telecommunications offices.
duties: Answer lines, patch connections, monitor trunk busy signals, and assist callers with long-distance bookings.
skills: Manual dexterity, polite diction, rapid recall of codes, stress tolerance.
tools: Cord switchboards, headsets, call logs.
regions: Urban telephone exchanges worldwide, 1890s–1970s.

events.label: Electronic switching
events.description: Automated exchanges reduce manual cord boards in wealthy markets.

name: Web Developer
summary: A builder of websites and web applications who implements interfaces and server logic for browsers—distinct from general software roles in its HTTP-centric stack.
duties: Implement UI components, build APIs, optimize performance, fix cross-browser bugs, and deploy sites.
skills: HTML/CSS/JS, frameworks, accessibility, version control, basic security hygiene.
tools: VS Code, Git, browsers, Node or edge runtimes, design handoff tools.
regions: Global remote workforce since the 1990s commercial web.
originLabel: Early public web after Mosaic browser era
educationPath: CS degrees, bootcamps, self-taught portfolios.
modernSectors: Agencies, SaaS, e-commerce, media, freelance.

events.label: AJAX and Web 2.0
events.description: Rich client apps blur line between documents and software on the web.

events.label: Mobile-first web
events.description: Responsive design becomes standard as smartphone traffic surges.

sources.title: Web history timelines
sources.note: Origin anchored to public web standards era.
